senses.definition: Third person singular form of a rare verb meaning to cause athetosis or to treat as involving athetosis.
examples: The neurologist athetizes the movements when describing the child’s symptoms.
In older case reports, the writer sometimes athetizes any slow, writhing movements.
The specialist athetizes these hand motions as part of cerebral palsy.

senses.definition: Present participle form of a rare verb meaning to cause athetosis or to treat as involving athetosis.
examples: The doctor is athetizing the child’s abnormal posture as part of a movement disorder.
Early neurologists were athetizing many forms of slow, twisting motion.
By athetizing these limb movements, the researcher narrows the diagnosis.

senses.definition: Relating to or showing athetosis, with slow, writhing, involuntary movements of the body.
examples: The child has athetoid movements in her hands and feet.
Athetoid cerebral palsy often makes fine motor tasks very difficult.
The therapist designs exercises for patients with athetoid posture.

senses.definition: A person who has athetoid movements, usually because of a neurological condition.
examples: The clinic provides long‑term support for athetosis patients and other athetoids.
An athetoid may need help with everyday tasks like writing or eating.

senses.definition: Plural of athetoid, meaning people who have athetoid movements because of a neurological condition.
examples: The new rehabilitation program was designed especially for athetoids.
Athetoids often benefit from customized assistive devices.

senses.definition: Plural of athetosis, meaning more than one case or type of the movement disorder with slow, writhing, involuntary movements.
examples: Different athetoses can arise from different kinds of brain injury.
Researchers compared several athetoses in children with cerebral palsy.

senses.definition: Showing or related to athetosis, with slow and writhing movements.
examples: The patient’s hand showed athetosic activity during the examination.
Athetosic patterns often appear in long‑term cerebral palsy.

senses.definition: A movement disorder with slow, continuous, involuntary, twisting or writhing movements, often in the hands, feet, arms, or legs.
examples: Athetosis can make it hard for a person to hold objects or keep a steady posture.
The doctor explained that athetosis comes from damage to certain areas of the brain.
Children with athetosis may need long‑term physical therapy.

senses.definition: Having the features of athetosis, with slow, twisting, involuntary movements.
examples: The child displayed athetotic movements in her fingers and toes.
Athetotic patterns can vary greatly between patients.

senses.definition: A very rare, dialect or poetic spelling of "a thing", meaning a single object, fact, or idea.
examples: He never forgot athing that she said that day.
Not athing was left after the storm passed.
There is athing about that house that makes me uneasy.

senses.definition: An obsolete or dialect form meaning to think or to seem to think.
examples: I athink he will not come before nightfall.
She athink it best to keep quiet.
They athink the journey too long and dangerous.

senses.definition: Very eager or hungry for something, especially for knowledge, power, or experience.
examples: Young researchers are athirst for new discoveries.
The people were athirst for change after many years of conflict.
She was athirst for adventure and travel.

senses.definition: A rare or Latinized form of the word "athlete", meaning a person trained in physical sports.
examples: The inscription honored an athleta who won many races.
In the old text, the word athleta refers to a champion fighter.
Each athleta in the games received a crown of leaves.

senses.definition: Plural of a rare or Latinized form "athleta", meaning several athletes or competitors in sports.
examples: The stone shows the names of several athletas who took part in the games.
In the epic, the athletas gather before the king.
Victorious athletas were celebrated in their home cities.

senses.definition: A person who is trained or very good at sports or physical exercise.
examples: The athlete trained every day for the Olympic Games.
Young athletes need a healthy diet and enough sleep.
She became a professional athlete when she was only nineteen.

senses.definition: The state or condition of being an athlete.
examples: He embraced athletehood with full dedication and discipline.
Athletehood often demands many personal sacrifices.
Her athletehood defined her teenage years.

senses.definition: Plural of athlete, meaning several people who are trained or very good at sports or physical exercise.
examples: The athletes waited nervously before the race began.
Many athletes from around the world came to the championship.
Young athletes often look up to famous sports stars.

senses.definition: Strong, fit, and good at sports or physical exercise.
examples: She has a very athletic body because she swims every day.
He looked tall and athletic in his running clothes.
The school is known for its athletic students.

senses.definition: Relating to athletics or organized sports activities.
examples: The university has excellent athletic facilities.
Athletic programs receive a lot of support at this college.
He works in the athletic department.

senses.definition: An old or rare form of "athletic", meaning strong, active, or related to sports.
examples: The poem praised his athletical grace and speed.
They admired the athletical games of the ancient world.
Her athletical manner impressed the crowd.

senses.definition: In a way that shows physical strength, fitness, or skill in sports.
examples: She moves athletically on the basketball court.
The team is athletically strong but lacks experience.
He has always performed athletically despite his small size.

senses.definition: The combination of physical qualities, such as speed, strength, and agility, that make someone good at sports.
examples: His natural athleticism helped him succeed in many sports.
The coach praised her athleticism and determination.
Good athleticism is important, but tactics also matter.

senses.definition: Plural of a rare form "athleticism", meaning different types or displays of athletic ability.
examples: The book describes the various athleticisms seen in different sports.
Modern games celebrate both mental skills and athleticisms.
Their performances showed many distinct athleticisms.

senses.definition: Sports and physical exercise in general, especially in schools and colleges.
examples: He decided to focus on athletics instead of music.
The school spends more money on athletics than on arts.
Athletics plays a big role in campus life.

senses.definition: Track and field events, such as running, jumping, and throwing, considered as a sport.
examples: She competes in athletics at the national level.
Athletics includes events like the 100‑meter race and the long jump.
Our club offers training in athletics for teenagers.

senses.definition: A rare variant of "athleticism", meaning physical strength and ability in sports.
examples: His athletism was clear from his powerful jump.
The scouts were impressed by her athletism.
Old reports praised the athletism of the team.

senses.definition: A humorous or critical term for a society or system where athletes have great power or influence.
examples: Some critics describe the country’s sports culture as a kind of athletocracy.
In this athletocracy, star players can even influence political decisions.
The article warns about the rise of an athletocracy in modern media.

senses.definition: A very rare or historical term related to officials who judged or supervised athletic contests in ancient games.
examples: The athlothete announced the winner of the race.
Each athlothete was respected for fairness and honor.
The inscription mentions an athlothete who served the city for many years.

senses.definition: Plural of a very rare or historical term "athlothete", meaning several officials who judged or supervised athletic contests.
examples: The athlothetes prepared the stadium for the games.
In ancient festivals, the athlothetes were chosen from noble families.
The city honored the athlothetes with special seats.

senses.definition: A type of jet engine that is basically a long tube where air enters at the front, fuel burns inside, and the hot gases leave at the back to produce thrust.
examples: The engineer explained how the athodyd works in supersonic missiles.
Unlike some engines, an athodyd has no major moving parts.
Early experiments with the athodyd led to modern ramjet designs.

senses.definition: Plural of athodyd, referring to more than one jet engine of this tube‑shaped type.
examples: The new missiles are powered by advanced athodyds.
Designers compared several athodyds before choosing the final model.
Different athodyds were tested in the wind tunnel.

senses.definition: A very rare or possibly erroneous form, probably intended as a technical term ending in "‑gen"; its meaning is unclear in contemporary English.
examples: The word athogen appears only in a few old technical lists.
Without context, no one could say what athogen really meant.
The scientist removed athogen from the revised terminology.

senses.definition: An extremely rare or dialect form, likely related to "a‑hold" meaning in a state of holding onto something; its usage is unclear.
examples: The sailor kept himself athold of the rope in the storm.
She stayed athold of her small bag all night.
They were athold of each other’s hands.

senses.definition: A monk or person from Mount Athos, a famous monastic region in Greece.
examples: An Athonite described the quiet life of prayer on the mountain.
The pilgrims listened to the Athonite with respect.
Many Athonites follow strict spiritual rules.

senses.definition: Relating to Mount Athos or its monastic communities.
examples: The Athonite tradition has influenced Orthodox spirituality.
He studies Athonite manuscripts in the library.
Athonite customs are very strict.

senses.definition: A Scots or dialect form meaning across.
examples: He stepped athort the narrow stream.
The bridge runs athort the valley.
They built fences athort the field.

senses.definition: Severe weakness and failure to grow in infants or children caused by lack of food and poor living conditions.
examples: The doctor diagnosed the child with athrepsia due to long‑term neglect.
Athrepsia can be prevented with proper nutrition and hygiene.
Many cases of athrepsia occur in areas of extreme poverty.

senses.definition: Relating to poor nutrition or failure to grow, especially in medical descriptions of children.
examples: The report described athreptic changes in the infant’s body.
Athreptic conditions are common where food is scarce.
The clinic specializes in treating athreptic children.

senses.definition: Filled with a strong feeling of excitement or emotion.
examples: She stood athrill with joy at the good news.
The audience was athrill as the singer walked on stage.
He felt athrill with fear and excitement.

senses.definition: An archaic form meaning to prosper or grow well.
examples: The crops did athrive in the rich soil.
He prayed that his family would athrive in the new land.
Nothing could athrive in such darkness.

senses.definition: Filled with or showing a strong beating or pulsing, especially of the heart or emotions.
examples: She waited athrob with anxiety.
His temples were athrob after the long run.
The city lay athrob with life and noise.

senses.definition: A very rare medical term for a cell that takes in or stores foreign particles in the body.
examples: Under the microscope, the athrocyte appeared full of pigment.
The tissue sample showed several athrocytes near the damaged area.
Researchers studied how each athrocyte reacted to the injected material.

senses.definition: Plural of athrocyte, meaning several cells that take in or store foreign particles in the body.
examples: The sample contained many athrocytes around the lesion.
Athrocytes can accumulate different types of material.
Scientists identified the athrocytes by their dark appearance.

senses.definition: Plural of athrocytosis, meaning several conditions in which cells store too much foreign material.
examples: Different athrocytoses have been described in the medical literature.
The doctor compared two rare athrocytoses affecting the liver.
Studies of athrocytoses can help us understand cell storage diseases.

senses.definition: A condition in which certain cells store or hold a lot of foreign or waste material.
examples: Athrocytosis can lead to visible deposits in organs.
The pathologist noted signs of athrocytosis in the spleen.
Researchers are trying to understand how athrocytosis develops.

senses.definition: A rare medical term meaning caused by or related to joints.
examples: The pain was thought to be athrogenic rather than muscular.
Athrogenic factors can limit an older person’s movement.
The study focused on athrogenic causes of disability.

senses.definition: An archaic or dialect form meaning to crowd together or to throng.
examples: People did athrong the streets during the festival.
They athronged the hall to hear the famous speaker.
Children athronged about the stage.

senses.definition: An archaic form meaning through.
examples: He passed athrough the dark forest.
The river runs athrough the valley.
They walked athrough the narrow gate.

senses.definition: A medical or psychological term for a strong loss of spirit or courage, often showing as deep sadness or lack of interest.
examples: The patient showed signs of athumia after the long illness.
Prolonged stress can lead to athumia and low motivation.
The doctor noted that athumia affected his recovery.

senses.definition: Across something, especially in a way that blocks or goes against it.
examples: A fallen tree lay athwart the path and stopped our progress.
The ship drifted athwart the narrow channel.
A long shadow fell athwart the doorway.

senses.definition: In a position across or against something.
examples: The logs lay athwart, making it hard to pass.
Her body fell athwart on the bed.
The boat lay athwart, blocking the harbor entrance.

senses.definition: In a position across the ship’s hawse, that is, across the front part where the anchor cables run.
examples: The small boat lay athwarthawse and risked damaging the anchor chain.
They warned the captain that another vessel was athwarthawse.
A ship drifting athwarthawse can quickly get into danger.

senses.definition: Across a ship from one side to the other, not along its length.
examples: The benches were fixed athwartship for better balance.
Water moved athwartship as the boat rolled.
They installed seats athwartship to save space.

senses.definition: Across a ship from side to side; another form of "athwartship".
examples: Cargo was secured athwartships to prevent shifting.
The wind blew athwartships, making steering difficult.
Hammocks hung athwartships in the sailors’ quarters.

senses.definition: Across something from side to side; at a right angle to its length.
examples: The poles were laid athwartwise over the trench.
She cut the bread athwartwise into thick slices.
The beams run athwartwise under the floor.
